content: Audio sampling fundamentals: CD quality is 44.1 kHz sample rate (Nyquist theorem requires 2x the highest frequency — human hearing tops at 20 kHz). Professional video uses 48 kHz. High-resolution audio uses 96 kHz or 192 kHz. Bit depth determines dynamic range: 16-bit gives 96 dB, 24-bit gives 144 dB (professional standard for recording and mixing).

Digital audio file sizes: 1 minute of uncompressed stereo 44.1kHz/16-bit PCM = 10.1 MB. 1 minute at 48kHz/24-bit = 16.6 MB. A 3-minute song: WAV ~30 MB, FLAC ~20 MB (lossless compression, 30-50% smaller), MP3 320kbps ~7.2 MB, AAC 256kbps ~5.8 MB, Opus 128kbps ~2.9 MB (transparent quality at half the bitrate of MP3).

FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) converts time-domain audio into frequency-domain representation in O(n log n) time vs O(n^2) for naive DFT. Window size (typically 1024, 2048, or 4096 samples) determines frequency resolution: 44100 Hz / 2048 = 21.5 Hz per bin. Larger windows give better frequency resolution but worse time resolution (uncertainty principle).

STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) applies FFT to overlapping windowed segments (hop_size = window_size/4 typical) producing a spectrogram. The mel spectrogram warps the frequency axis to the mel scale (1000 mel = 1000 Hz reference), which approximates human pitch perception. 80-128 mel bands from 0-8000 Hz is standard for speech/music analysis.

MFCC (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients) are the standard features for speech recognition and speaker identification. Computation: audio → pre-emphasis → windowed frames → FFT → mel filterbank → log → DCT → 13-20 MFCCs. Delta and delta-delta MFCCs capture temporal dynamics. Total feature vector: 39 dimensions (13 static + 13 delta + 13 delta-delta) per frame.

CTC (Connectionist Temporal Classification) loss enables end-to-end speech recognition without pre-segmented alignments. CTC adds a blank token and marginalizes over all valid alignments. DeepSpeech 2 uses CTC with bidirectional RNNs achieving 5.6% WER on LibriSpeech clean. CTC assumes conditional independence between output tokens — attention-based models (Whisper) handle this better.

Attention-based speech recognition: Encoder-decoder with attention (Listen, Attend and Spell) processes spectrograms and generates text autoregressively. OpenAI Whisper uses a Transformer encoder-decoder trained on 680,000 hours of multilingual audio, achieving 2-5% WER on English and supporting 99 languages. Whisper large-v3 has 1.5B parameters and processes audio in 30-second chunks.

Text-to-speech evolution: Tacotron 2 (2017) generates mel spectrograms from text using seq2seq with attention, then WaveNet vocoder converts to audio at 24 kHz. VITS (2021) is end-to-end with adversarial training, producing 22.05 kHz audio in real-time. XTTS v2 (Coqui) supports voice cloning from 6 seconds of reference audio in 16 languages.

Modern TTS architecture: Text encoder (phoneme/character embeddings) → Duration predictor → Acoustic decoder (mel spectrogram) → Neural vocoder (waveform). Vocoders: WaveNet (autoregressive, slow), WaveRNN (10x faster), HiFi-GAN (real-time, GAN-based, 22 kHz). FastSpeech 2 uses non-autoregressive generation for 100x speedup over Tacotron 2 with comparable quality.

Music generation with AI: Diffusion models (Stable Audio, Riffusion) generate spectrograms from text prompts, then invert to audio. Transformer-based: MusicGen (Meta, 3.3B parameters) generates 32 kHz stereo from text/melody conditioning. MusicLM (Google) uses hierarchical tokens. Jukebox (OpenAI) generates raw audio at 44.1 kHz including vocals, using VQ-VAE with 3 levels of compression.

Audio tokenization for language models: EnCodec (Meta) compresses 24 kHz audio to 1.5-24 kbps using residual vector quantization (RVQ) with 8 codebooks. SoundStream (Google) achieves similar compression. These tokens enable treating audio generation as a language modeling task. AudioLM generates audio by predicting semantic tokens (w2v-BERT) then acoustic tokens (SoundStream).

CLAP (Contrastive Language-Audio Pretraining) learns joint audio-text embeddings, analogous to CLIP for images. Trained on 633K audio-text pairs, CLAP achieves zero-shot audio classification by comparing audio embeddings with text embeddings of class descriptions. Applications: audio search by text query, audio captioning, and zero-shot sound event detection.

Active Noise Cancellation (ANC) uses microphones to capture ambient noise, then generates an anti-phase signal (180-degree phase inversion) played through speakers to cancel the noise. Feedforward ANC uses external mic (better for consistent noise), feedback ANC uses internal mic (compensates for imperfect cancellation). Effective for low frequencies <1 kHz; passive isolation handles higher frequencies.

ANC performance metrics: Top headphones (Sony WH-1000XM5, Apple AirPods Max) achieve 25-30 dB noise reduction at 100-500 Hz. Adaptive ANC adjusts filter coefficients in real-time using LMS (Least Mean Squares) algorithm with step size mu=0.01-0.1. Hybrid ANC combines feedforward and feedback for broadband cancellation. Latency must be <1ms for effective cancellation at voice frequencies.

Spatial audio uses HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) to simulate 3D sound positioning through headphones. HRTF captures how sound from a specific direction is filtered by the head, ears, and torso — unique per person. Generic HRTFs work for 70% of listeners; personalized HRTFs (ear scanning) improve localization accuracy by 30-40%. Apple Spatial Audio uses head-tracking + generic HRTFs.

Ambisonics encodes 3D sound fields using spherical harmonics. First-order ambisonics uses 4 channels (W, X, Y, Z) for basic 3D; third-order uses 16 channels for precise localization. Ambisonic recording uses a tetrahedral microphone array (Sennheiser AMBEO, Zoom H3-VR). Decoded to any speaker layout or binaural via HRTF convolution for VR/AR applications.

MP3 compression uses perceptual coding: the psychoacoustic model identifies masked frequencies (quieter sounds near louder ones) and removes them. Critical bands divide the frequency spectrum into 32 subbands with MDCT. Bit allocation prioritizes audible content. At 128 kbps, MP3 removes ~90% of data with noticeable artifacts; 320 kbps is considered transparent quality for most listeners.

Opus codec delivers the best quality-per-bit for real-time communication. It combines SILK (speech, 6-40 kbps) and CELT (music, 6-510 kbps) codecs with seamless switching. At 64 kbps, Opus matches MP3 at 128 kbps. Latency as low as 2.5ms (5ms typical). Native support in WebRTC, Discord, and all modern browsers. Opus 1.4+ uses neural network for packet loss concealment.

Beat detection algorithms: Onset detection identifies the start of musical events using spectral flux (difference between consecutive FFT frames). Threshold the onset detection function to find beat candidates. Tempo estimation: autocorrelation of onset function or comb filter bank testing BPM range (60-200). Librosa in Python: librosa.beat.beat_track() returns estimated tempo and beat frame positions.

Tempo estimation accuracy: librosa.beat.beat_track achieves 85-95% accuracy on standard datasets (GTZAN, Ballroom). The predominant local pulse (PLP) method handles tempo changes better than global methods. Common music tempos: Classical 60-120 BPM, Pop 100-130 BPM, House 120-130 BPM, Drum & Bass 160-180 BPM. Time signature detection requires additional downbeat tracking.

Voice cloning creates a synthetic replica of a person's voice from a short audio sample. VALL-E (Microsoft) clones from 3 seconds of audio using neural codec language modeling. Tortoise-TTS produces high-quality clones from 5-30 seconds. Speaker embedding extracts a 256-dimensional vector (d-vector or x-vector) capturing speaker characteristics independent of content.

Speaker verification compares two voice embeddings using cosine similarity: score = cos(embed_A, embed_B). Equal Error Rate (EER) where false accept = false reject is the standard metric — state-of-art EER is 0.5-1.5% on VoxCeleb. ECAPA-TDNN architecture achieves 0.87% EER with 6M parameters. Applications: voice biometrics, speaker diarization (who spoke when).

Audio source separation isolates individual instruments or voices from a mix. Demucs v4 (Meta) achieves state-of-art separation into 4 stems (vocals, drums, bass, other) with 7.3 dB SDR improvement on musdb18. Uses hybrid architecture: temporal convolution + Transformer. Real-time separation possible at 44.1 kHz on modern GPUs. Applications: karaoke, remixing, transcription.

Audio data augmentation for ML training: Time stretch (0.8-1.2x speed without pitch change), Pitch shift (+/- 2 semitones), Add background noise (SNR 5-20 dB), Room impulse response convolution (simulate reverb), SpecAugment (mask frequency bands and time steps in spectrogram). These augmentations improve model robustness by 10-30% on out-of-domain test sets.

Audio fingerprinting (Shazam algorithm): Convert audio to spectrogram, find constellation points (local maxima in frequency-time), form pairs of points as landmarks, hash landmark features (frequencies + time delta). A 5-second clip generates ~500 hashes. Database lookup matches hashes with time alignment verification. Shazam's database contains 50M+ tracks and identifies songs in 1-3 seconds.
